macro_segments.title: Introduction to Borrower-Lender Mitzvot and Torah Sources

micro_segments.title: Overview of daf 113 and transition from employer-employee to borrower-lender relationships
Five primary mitzvot from Deuteronomy 24: widow's garment, entering the home, standing outside, poor person's collateral, millstone
Rambam's categorization of mitzvot in Hilchos Malveh VeLoveh and distinction between widow (all cases) and others
Debate between Rambam and Ra'avad: whether restrictions on collateral apply at time of loan or only after default
Power relationship between lender and borrower as central concern of the mitzvot
Menachem Elon's meshpat ivri framework and the distinction between obligations and rights
Prohibitions specific to millstone and tools of livelihood; lo tihiyeh lo k'nosheh on exerting power over poor debtor

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Collateral Seizure and the Role of Beit Din

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: creditor cannot seize collateral except through Beit Din; cannot enter debtor's home
Case of two garments for poor person: taking one, leaving one; returning based on need
Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel: thirty-day limit on daily return of collateral; after that, collateral sold in Beit Din
Death of debtor ends obligation to return collateral; creditor may liquidate to collect loan
Timing clarification: seizure after loan default, not at time of loan; role of Beit Din in collection
Psychological benefit of daily return: reminder of debt, potential impact on Shemittah annulment

macro_segments.title: Gemara's First Challenge: Can Beit Din Enter the Home?

micro_segments.title: Shmuel: shliach Beit Din can seize in public but cannot enter home (lo memashkeni); debate over משנה interpretation
Gemara clarifies משנה: prohibition on creditor entering home; question whether Beit Din excepted
Rav Yosef's challenge: if lo yachavol reichayim varachev and lo tachavol beged almanah prohibit seizure, who is permitted to enter home?
Rav Papa's answer: multiple transgressions (lo taseh violations) if creditor enters for forbidden items, but doesn't prove Beit Din permitted

macro_segments.title: Textual Proofs on Beit Din's Authority: bachutz ta'amod and ve'ha'ish

micro_segments.title: Reading bachutz ta'amod: simple meaning (creditor stands outside, debtor brings collateral) versus derasha implications
Ve'ha'ish as surplus word: le'rabos shliach Beit Din to show someone other than creditor can bring collateral
Baraita: im chavo tachavol - if collateral taken (implying entry), must return before sunset; chaluka on whether creditor or Beit Din took it
First baraita: shliach Beit Din can enter home to seize collateral; lo tavo applies to creditor only
Second baraita: even shliach Beit Din must stand outside; neither creditor nor Beit Din enters; only debtor brings collateral out

macro_segments.title: Collateral Restrictions and Basic Needs Framework

micro_segments.title: Prohibition on taking reichayim varachev (millstone parts) and tools of livelihood; applies to all persons (not just poor)
Second baraita's detailed rules: two beds and mattress for rich person; two beds and mat for poor person; only for debtor himself, not family
Gemara: why two beds? One for eating/reclining (Roman custom), one for sleeping; allows debtor to walk four cubits before sleep (health concern)
Mesadrin concept: arranging debtor's basic needs parallel to erchin (vows) framework in Torah
Erchin example: kohein assesses what debtor can afford based on ability to pay; determines basic needs first, then obligation

macro_segments.title: Reconciling Mesadrin with Liquidation: Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel's Thirty-Day Rule

micro_segments.title: Challenge: how can Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel say both mesadrin (leave basic needs) and sell collateral (after 30 days)?
First resolution: selling collateral does not mean leaving debtor nothing; can sell luxury items, keep basic necessities
Objection: if Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel holds bnei melachim (all Jews are royalty), everything could be deemed 'basic necessity'
Abaye's principle: Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel, Rabbi Shimon, Rabbi Yishmael, Rabbi Akiva all hold kol Yisrael bnei melachim hein
Implications: cannot downgrade living standard or replace luxuries with necessities; all entitled to royal treatment
Unresolved tension: how to reconcile daily return obligation, mesadrin (basic needs), and 30-day liquidation under bnei melachim framework

topical_tags.term: lo tavo el beito la'avot avoto (do not enter his house to take collateral)
lo tachavol beged almanah (do not take widow's garment)
lo yachavol reichayim varachev (do not take millstone)
devarim she'osim bahem ochel nefesh (items for sustenance/livelihood)
lo tihiyeh lo k'nosheh (do not be like a creditor/exert power)
shliach Beit Din (agent of the court)
lo yimshaknennu ela b'Beit Din (cannot take collateral except through court)
bnei melachim hein (all Jews are royal descendants)
im ish ani hu (if he is poor)
lo tishkav ba'avoto (do not keep/sleep with collateral)
